Mr. Don Hyatt 
L.E.A.P. Committee 
Boilermakers Local Union No. 92 
4809 East Firestone Boulevard 
South Gate, California 90280 
Dear Mr. Hyatt: 
June 4, 1981 
This letter is to verify my advice to you during our 
telephone conversation of May 29, 1981 concerning the filing 
obligations of your Union's International L.E.A.P. Committee and 
a proposed fundraiser sponsored by your local here in California. 
The facts as I understand them are as follows: 
1. 
The California Local 92 of Boilermakers is considering 
holding a fundraiser in the Southern California area 
some time in the near future. 
2. 
All of the money raised will be sent to the International 
Union and allocated for California political contributions. 
3. 
The International Union has, or will be filing as a 
recipient committee with the Secretary of State of 
California, and filing the required periodic campaign 
statements. 
4. 
Local 92 does not intend to raise political 
nor make political expenditures on its own. 
contributions and expenditures will be made 
International Union. 
contributions 
All 
by the 
The question you raised was whether Local 92 could open a 
special bank checking account in order to make the deposits of 
contributions received from the fundraiser and to make any 
necessary expenditures required rather than having to wait for 
checks and signatures from the International Union which is 
out-of-state? 
The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted a 
regulation which establishes the guidelines for an organization 
sponsoring a political action committee such as International

Union of Boilermakers. 
If the criteria established by 
is 
regulation is met, Local 92 of the,Boilermakers in California 
would not be required to file as a separate recipient COIT~ittee. 
Local 92 would be acting as an "Intermediate unit" for the 
International's PAC (2 Cal. Adm. l8419(d». 
I have enclosed 
a copy of this regulation for your information. 
Local 92 would have to report to the International's 
PAC those contributors who purchased tickets to the fundraiser 
and all of the expenditures made relating to the fundraiser. 
The International PAC would then renort Local 92 as the 
Intermediarv for those contributors'who contributed a hundred 
dollars ($100) or more which would have to be itemized on the 
PAC's campaign statement. 
In other words, the International's 
PAC is required to file campaign statements and report all of 
the transactions for the fundraiser in California as if the PAC 
had received the contributions and made the expenditures directly. 
I hope this information is helpful and please feel free to 
contact our office if we can be of any further assistance.

sections.question: Whether Local 92 could open a special bank checking account in order to make the deposits of contributions received from the fundraiser and to make any necessary expenditures required rather than having to wait for checks and signatures from the International Union which is out-of-state?
sections.conclusion: If the criteria established by regulation is met, Local 92 of the Boilermakers in California would not be required to file as a separate recipient committee. Local 92 would be acting as an 'Intermediate unit' for the International's PAC (2 Cal. Adm. §18419(d)). Local 92 would have to report to the International's PAC those contributors who purchased tickets to the fundraiser and all of the expenditures made relating to the fundraiser. The International PAC would then report Local 92 as the Intermediary for those contributors who contributed a hundred dollars ($100) or more which would have to be itemized on the PAC's campaign statement.
sections.question_synthetic: Can Local 92 of Boilermakers open a separate bank account to receive and manage fundraiser contributions for the International Union's PAC without establishing itself as a separate recipient committee?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Local 92 may operate as an 'Intermediate unit' under FPPC regulation without filing as a separate recipient committee, provided it reports all fundraiser contributors and expenditures to the International Union's PAC. The International PAC must then report Local 92's transactions as if the PAC received contributions and made expenditures directly.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear facts, question, and conclusion sections. Document dated June 4, 1981. Regulation reference 18419(d) enclosed. No withdrawal or declination elements present.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Local 92 could open a special bank checking account in order to make the deposits of contributions received from the fundraiser and to make any necessary expenditures required rather than having to wait for checks and signatures from the International Union which is out-of-state?

CONCLUSION: If the criteria established by regulation is met, Local 92 of the Boilermakers in California would not be required to file as a separate recipient committee. Local 92 would be acting as an 'Intermediate unit' for the International's PAC (2 Cal. Adm. §18419(d)). Local 92 would have to report to the International's PAC those contributors who purchased tickets to the fundraiser and all of the expenditures made relating to the fundraiser. The International PAC would then report Local 92 as the Intermediary for those contributors who contributed a hundred dollars ($100) or more which would have to be itemized on the PAC's campaign statement.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Local 92 of Boilermakers that it may establish a bank account for a fundraiser benefiting the International Union's PAC without registering as a separate recipient committee if it operates as an 'Intermediate unit' and reports all contributions and expenditures to the International PAC.
